pesach_rice.ruling: Rice is permitted on Pesach for Kafkazi Jews, following Edot HaMizrach custom. However, many Kafkazi families do NOT eat rice on the first two days of Pesach, possibly out of respect for Ashkenazic guests they would host.
pesach_rice.notes: Some families are stricter than others. Ask your family's posek.

pesach_restrictions.ruling: Kafkazi Jews are traditionally strict on Pesach, avoiding pickled foods, chocolate, and most commercially produced items beyond basic staples.
pesach_restrictions.notes: A sheep is traditionally slaughtered before Pesach, with meat shared among the poor.

govgil.ruling: Govgil is a traditional Kafkazi end-of-Pesach community picnic celebration involving the entire community gathering outdoors.
govgil.notes: Unique to Mountain Jewish communities. Not found in other traditions.

general_pesach.ruling: Kafkazi Jews follow Edot HaMizrach Pesach customs generally, with Persian-influenced traditions. Tara stew (chard, meat, rice) is a traditional Pesach dish.
general_pesach.notes: Juhuri-language piyyutim may be recited at the Seder in some families.

kashrut_general.ruling: Kafkazi Jews follow Sephardic kashrut standards. Lamb is the traditional meat. Many families historically had their own shochet in the community.
kashrut_general.notes: In Brooklyn, most Kafkazi Jews use Sephardic-certified products.

shabbat.ruling: Kafkazi Shabbat follows Edot HaMizrach customs with Persian-influenced piyyutim and Juhuri songs. Hospitality (hachnasat orchim) is a central Shabbat value — a special guest room (for kunaks) was traditionally maintained.
shabbat.notes: Strong Kabbalistic influence in Shabbat prayers and customs.

marriage.ruling: Kafkazi wedding celebrations span multiple days. The groom's family brings gifts (on trays called tabakho) to the bride in a musical procession. The bride traditionally moves into the groom's father's house.
marriage.notes: Engagement is marked by the groom's friends gifting the bride silk headscarves.

rabbinate.ruling: Kafkazi Jews historically maintained a two-tiered rabbinate: a Rabbi (maggid/cantor/teacher) and a Dayan (halachic decisor). For practical halachic questions, a Dayan's ruling was sought.
rabbinate.notes: In 19th century, many young Kafkazi men studied in Lithuanian yeshivot and returned as rabbis, strengthening halachic observance.

mysticism.ruling: Mountain Jewish traditions are deeply infused with Kabbalah and Jewish mysticism, inherited from Persian Jewish roots and reinforced through contact with Sephardic kabbalistic traditions.
mysticism.notes: This kabbalistic influence appears in prayers, lifecycle events, and daily customs.

pesach_rice.ruling: Bukharian Jews permit rice and kitniyot on Pesach, following Sephardic/Edot HaMizrach ruling of Rav Yosef Karo.
pesach_rice.source: Shulchan Arukh, Orach Chaim 453 / Sephardic custom
pesach_rice.notes: No restriction on the first two days, unlike some Kafkazi families.

general.ruling: Bukharian Jews follow Sephardic halacha (Ben Ish Chai, Kaf HaChaim) with Central Asian cultural customs. Strong Persian and Silk Road influences in liturgy and food.
general.notes: Large Bukharian community in Queens, NY (Rego Park/Forest Hills area).

kitniyot.ruling: Sephardic Jews permit kitniyot (rice, legumes, corn, soy) on Pesach per the Shulchan Arukh (OC 453). This is the ruling of Rav Yosef Karo and is followed by Ben Ish Chai and Kaf HaChaim.
kitniyot.source: Shulchan Arukh OC 453, Ben Ish Chai, Kaf HaChaim
kitniyot.notes: Even Sephardim must check rice carefully for chametz grains.

kitniyot.ruling: Ashkenazic Jews prohibit kitniyot on Pesach by rabbinic decree (minhag). This includes rice, beans, corn, soy, and their derivatives. The Rema (OC 453) codifies this custom.
kitniyot.source: Rema on Shulchan Arukh OC 453, Mishnah Berurah
kitniyot.notes: Some Ashkenazic authorities permit kitniyot oil. Consult your rabbi.
